error.signature: AI tells a traveler to Singapore to pack chewing gum for the flight or casually gift Singaporean colleagues a pack

verdict.summary: Singapore's Regulation of Imports and Exports Act (Chewing Gum Import Control) prohibits importing, selling, and distributing chewing gum — a ban dating from 1992 after MRT door sensors were repeatedly jammed with gum. Since 2004, only therapeutic/dental gum is sold in pharmacies with prescription. Personal consumption is not criminal, but import quantities or sale are. Additional strict bans/penalties: vaping (fully illegal since 2018), drug offenses (death penalty for trafficking), littering (heavy fines), jaywalking, smoking outside designated areas.

dead_ends.action: Bring a box of chewing gum into Singapore as a gift or for personal resale
dead_ends.why_fails: Customs seizes gum at Changi. Importing 'for trade' (commercial quantity) is prosecutable — up to S$100,000 fine and 2 years imprisonment under Regulation of Imports and Exports Act. Small personal amounts for own use have historically been tolerated in carry-on but not guaranteed.

dead_ends.action: Vape (e-cigarette) in Singapore, even in private or in a hotel room
dead_ends.why_fails: Singapore's Tobacco (Control of Advertisements and Sale) Act bans import, sale, and use of e-vaporisers since 2018. Using/possessing in public can incur fines up to S$2,000 (first offense); importing carries up to 6 months jail + S$10,000 fine. Enforcement is active; customs scans inbound luggage.

dead_ends.action: Transit through Changi with any amount of cannabis, cocaine, MDMA, or other Class A drugs — even in checked luggage, even in transit to a third country
dead_ends.why_fails: Misuse of Drugs Act imposes death penalty for trafficking above threshold amounts: 15g heroin, 30g cocaine, 500g cannabis, 250g MA (methamphetamine). Transit does not exempt — possession on Singapore soil counts. Multiple foreign nationals have been executed. Random drug tests on passengers exist.

workarounds.action: Don't import gum, vapes, or recreational drugs. For nicotine addiction, Singapore sells patches/gum for smoking cessation by prescription at polyclinics and some pharmacies (HPB-approved list).

General 'fine city' awareness: fines for littering (S$300–1000), eating/drinking on MRT (S$500), smoking outside designated yellow-box zones (S$200), jaywalking (S$20 on the spot), feeding pigeons (S$500). Follow signage strictly.

For long-stay residents: download the MyEnv app for waste management rules, OneMotoring app for vehicular, and LifeSG for general government services. Rules are clearly published; enforcement is consistent.
